turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: We'll hear argument next in Case 25-83, Jules versus Andre Balazs. Mr. Unikowsky.

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: In Badgerow versus Walters, this Court held that federal courts lack jurisdiction over applications under Sections 9 and 10 of the Federal Arbitration Act unless an independent jurisdictional basis appears on the face of the application. In this case, the Court should hold that Badgerow's rule applies to all Section 9 and 10 applications regardless of whether a preexisting suit is on file. Nothing in the FAA distinguishes between those Section 9 and 10 applications that are associated with a preexisting suit and those that are not. Moreover, Section 8 of the FAA contains explicit jurisdictional anchor language that Sections 9 and 10 lack. The Court should infer that this textual disparity is intentional, much as the Court relied on a similar inference in Badgerow. The Court should resolve this case based on the text of the FAA rather than based on Section 1367's "related to" standard, but if the Court reaches that issue, it should hold that there is no common nucleus of operative fact between the underlying suit and the Section 9 and 10 applications which concern an arbitration award that did not exist at the time of the original federal suit. As Badgerow put it, the original -- the underlying dispute is not now at issue. In Kokkonen, this Court held that a settlement dispute is insufficiently related to the underlying dispute to warrant the exercise of ancillary jurisdiction. And the Court should rely on similar reasoning here in rejecting the jurisdiction. I welcome the Court's questions.

turns.name: CLARENCE THOMAS
turns.text: Was there a pending federal case in Badgerow?

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: There was no pending federal case in Badgerow, and we --

turns.name: CLARENCE THOMAS
turns.text: So don't you think that's a difference?

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: Well, that is indeed a factual distinction between this case and Badgerow.

turns.name: CLARENCE THOMAS
turns.text: Okay. So your client brings the federal case, right?

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: CLARENCE THOMAS
turns.text: The court then enters a stay so that the arbitration can take place?

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: CLARENCE THOMAS
turns.text: The arbitration then is decided, and the parties come back to that very same court to confirm the arbitral award?

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: CLARENCE THOMAS
turns.text: Or decision. And you're saying now that the jurisdiction of the court that you invoked is inadequate to decide this?

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: That's correct, Your Honor. There is -- I'm sorry.

turns.name: CLARENCE THOMAS
turns.text: Don't you think there's something odd about that?

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: I don't think so. I think our position follows pretty cleanly.

turns.name: CLARENCE THOMAS
turns.text: You think that that's consistent with Badgerow?

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: Yes, I think that the Badgerow rule -- admittedly, in the Badgerow case, there was no original lawsuit, and in this case, there is. And that's why this is a separate case from Badgerow. But, ultimately, I think the same rule should apply.

turns.name: CLARENCE THOMAS
turns.text: So, in Badgerow, there was no jurisdiction, and so the Court said that there's no independent jurisdiction. And, here, you have a pending case in which the district court stayed its suit -- its action pending the arbitration, and you say there's -- that's similar to Badgerow, where there was no case?

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: No, it's a different case, Your Honor, but I think the same rule should still apply whether or not you start with the text of the FAA or whether you start with Section 1367, as Respondents would advocate. So maybe it's helpful if I start with the latter point before turning to the text --

turns.name: BRETT KAVANAUGH
turns.text: What's the purpose of the stay?

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: The purposes of the stay is for if the case comes back and is not resolved in arbitration. So, if you look at the text of Section 3, Section 3 says that if it turns out that the parties are in default of the arbitration, so the arbitration doesn't resolve the dispute, then the parties can come back to the federal court and adjudicate -- and the federal court can adjudicate the dispute. Or, in -- in the Spizzirri case, the Court offered the example of a case where the arbitration breaks down, and so the case is stayed and the case might have to be reactivated in federal court. But, here, there's an arbitration award. The arbitration has wrapped up. And so, under Rule 8 of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure, the Court should dismiss the federal suit. It's quite similar to the facts --

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Counsel, doesn't Rule 8(c)(1) say that an arbitration has to be pled as an affirmative defense?

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: Right. So I --

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: So why aren't we thinking of it that way? Meaning Rule 8 says in a litigation where an arbitration is sought. And that's what the federal action here was, an -- it was a federal suit. The defense was arbitration controls. Why isn't this just a motion to confirm that the claim can't go forward?

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: So I think that dismissal would be appropriate. And because, at the start of the lawsuit, there was --

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: No, but you're arguing something else. You're arguing that not dismissal is appropriate but confirming the arbitration award so it is final and settles the matter is not.

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: Right, our --

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Because that's what you're arguing.

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: We're arguing, Your Honor, that the Court doesn't have jurisdiction to confirm the award, as opposed to dismiss the federal case, because confirming the award is --

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Then how can you dismiss it if you don't know if the arbitration award will be sustained, confirmed?

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: Well, the vast majority of arbitration awards are never subject to confirm or vacate proceedings.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Well, but the point still remains, how does a court know that it's a defense to the action until it has decided that it's a valid award?

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: No, because the award itself is the defense. It functions like a release. So, if a -- if a plaintiff files a lawsuit and the defendant --

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Well, you can't claim an arbitration award that has been vacated, correct?

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: That's correct. So what a court could do -- so a court might not know if the arbitration award is going to be later subject to confirm or vacate proceedings in state court. That could take several years. And so what a court would ordinarily do is dismiss the case. Theoretically, if the award is vacated, they would typically rearbitrate it. They wouldn't necessarily go back to the federal district court. In a -- I'm sorry, Your Honor, do you have a question?

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: No, no. Counsel, I'm having trouble understanding your lawsuit because I thought Spizzirri answered it already. In Spizzirri, what we said was, where a court does have proper jurisdiction over a Section 9 or 10 application in a preexisting lawsuit -- and there was, under Section 3, a preexisting federal claim, so it had proper jurisdiction -- the court that stayed the action can and should, for efficiency's sake, adjudicate those applications as motions in the existing lawsuit. So this is a motion in an existing lawsuit -- that's what Section 9 and 10 are -- over a claim that had jurisdiction, age discrimination. Why isn't this just a motion to confirm that?

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: Well, Your Honor, so, in -- in Badgerow, the Court addressed the language in Section 6 about motions that Your Honor just referred to. The Court said that it's not a motion. It says that the court's going to treat the application as a motion for efficiency's sake. So I don't think that the language in Section 6 carries the day.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: But what Badgerow also said, it said something like jurisdiction to decide a case includes jurisdiction to decide a motion in a case. And isn't that what we have here? And Badgerow distinguished that from using the look-through method. It's just saying, if you have jurisdiction over a case, you have jurisdiction over the motions in the case. That seems pretty -- you know, just a truism. And isn't that what's involved here?

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: I don't think so because the court has jurisdiction to decide motions. It's just not necessarily every single motion that would result in the court entering a judgment that exceeds its jurisdiction, because the judgment that Respondents are asking the court to enter is not merely a judgment of dismissal, which, of course, the court can do. It's essentially transforming the arbitration award into a judgment which would allow Respondents to enlist the court to execute the judgment against --

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Well, so explain that. What category of motions should not be included in the statement of, if you have jurisdiction over a case, you have jurisdiction over the motions in a case? What category of motions is excluded from that?

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: Motions that would cause the court to enter a judgment that the court doesn't have jurisdiction to enter because, in this case, the court undoubtably had jurisdiction to dismiss Petitioner's suit based on the fact that an arbitration award existed. What the court didn't have jurisdiction to do is enter an award in what was essentially a breach-of-contract suit that was beyond the court's jurisdiction. So, absolutely, the court could have dismissed the claim, but we think there's a difference between dismissal of a claim and transforming what is fundamentally a contract dispute into a judgment when the court lacks jurisdiction over that case.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: But the question -- that's begging the question, I think, because isn't the purpose of Section 9 to give the court the ability to do that, to say that that's exactly what is supposed to happen? I mean, I -- I've been a little confused by some of the things -- the representations that you made. You suggested that the vast number of arbitrations are not subject to "confirm and vacate proceedings." And, I guess, to the extent that you're suggesting that there won't be a dispute about whether or not the award is to be confirmed, that's true. But, in most cases, when you have an arbitration, isn't there a circumstance in which some court, whether the federal court or the state court, is asked to convert the award into a judgment so that it can ultimately be enforced?

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: No, that's actually quite rare, Your Honor. The vast majority of arbitration awards do not result in subsequent judicial proceedings because it's so hard to vacate an arbitration award that in almost every case the parties adhere to the award. Usually, when the defendant wins the arbitration, you know, usually, the defendant doesn't come to court to confirm because the arbitration award itself generally has res judicata effect.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: All right. But, in a situation, this goes back to Justice Thomas's initial question in which we already have a preexisting lawsuit related to these parties, related to an underlying dispute that then gets resolved by arbitration, it seems peculiar, I think, that you would suggest that Congress set these rules up to insist that another court be involved in confirming that award so as to close out the initial case. I mean, the -- the -- the circumstances -- you -- you would, I think, have to agree that when we have a existing lawsuit that was stayed for the purpose of arbitration, it can't be one of the situations in which the arbitration can happen and everybody goes off into the sunset and the original court doesn't know what happened. So there has to be some closure to this. And I think, under your rule, the closure you're positing comes from another court confirming the award, and that just seems very odd.

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: I don't think so, Your Honor. So, first of all, I think that the closure happens when the award occurs and the parties come back to the federal court and say, look, we were able to resolve our dispute in arbitration. There's no more pending claims. There's no partial substantive claims that were stayed. The entire case is over.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: But you do agree they have to come back to the federal court? They can't just --

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: Of course, they have to come back to the federal -- of course.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: All right. And so -- but -- but somehow you're saying that having exercised jurisdiction over this matter from day one at your request, Justice Thomas reminds us, the only way it gets resolved from the purpose -- from the standpoint of wrapping this up is if the parties are just coming back to notify that another court has been engaged to do some sort of confirmation. Or you're saying the confirmation doesn't have to happen at all?

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: It doesn't have to happen at all. There's, like, a four-year statute of limitations at least in California on the confirmation proceedings. The court's not just going to sit there for four years to wait if -- to -- to see whether someone's going to try --

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: But, if the parties want it to happen --

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: -- you're saying they cannot ask the court that took original jurisdiction over it to be the one to confirm.

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: Right. That's correct. They should go to the state court, and the court is free to stay the dismissal of the case if it knows that there is ongoing confirmation or vacatur proceedings in another court and so -- but all the court cannot do is transform what is fundamentally a breach-of- contract suit into a judgment.

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: Mr. Unikowsky, why wouldn't there be ancillary -- ancillary enforcement jurisdiction to be specific of the -- of the kind that was -- that the Court talked about in Kokkonen? It seems to me that if the parties come back, you know, Kokkonen envisioned a situation in which the district court could, under 41, make it part of the terms of the settlement agreement that the district court could retain authority to enforce the agreement and that that wouldn't have been a problem. There would be no separate basis of jurisdiction there, right? It's a distinct action, it's a contract action in essence that's distinct from the underlying suit, wouldn't be 1331 or 1332, for example. Why couldn't something like that theory apply here?

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: So, first of all, that's not Respondents' argument, and that's not what the Court did in this case. The Section 3 order did not purport to retain jurisdiction to confirm a follow-up arbitration award. It just said pursuant to Section 3 that the case will be stayed until arbitration has been had. I also don't think a court could do that. I don't think a court could circumvent the limitations under Section 3 by entering an order that's broader than Section 3, especially given that you have Section 8, which explicitly authorizes the retention of jurisdiction in maritime cases, and Section 9 does not have similar language. I also think that a court doesn't have unlimited authority to retain jurisdiction under Rule 41. I think the analysis is somewhat similar to the analysis in cases involving consent decrees like -- so, in certain cases, a court can take a settlement and embody it in a judgment, but the court can't just rubber-stamp it. The court has to make an independent assessment of things like whether or not the settlement advances the objectives underlying the statute, and only then can it really give the settlement the imprimatur of the court's authority. And so we think that, you know, a similar analysis would have to happen here, except it can't because, in an arbitration award, the court really isn't considering on its own the viability of the award. It's just assessing whether the arbitrator exceeded its powers. So, in a nutshell, I don't think the court did that in the Section 3 stay. I don't think the court could have done that either in the Section 3 stay.

turns.name: BRETT KAVANAUGH
turns.text: When it comes back, if the case were a federal question case to begin with and it comes back, there's no federal question, therefore, it goes to state court, correct?

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: So what happens is the court -- yes. So there was a federal question at the outset.

turns.name: BRETT KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: And so the arbitration award functions like a release or res judicata. It's a defense saying this case has been resolved in a different forum.

turns.name: BRETT KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Got it. So the confirm or vacate, though, goes to state court in those cases?

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: BRETT KAVANAUGH
turns.text: If you come back and the original jurisdiction was based on diversity of citizenship, you still have the diversity when you come back? Can you --

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: Correct. The face of the application --

turns.name: BRETT KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Then you go to federal court?

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: You can, because there's a independent jurisdictional basis on the face --

turns.name: BRETT KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Which it just strikes me as odd that if the original case to Justice Thomas's question was based on federal question jurisdiction, you'd end up in state court to confirm or vacate whereas, if the original jurisdiction was based on diversity and that still exists at the end, you would end up in federal court. It seems like an odd result.

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: I don't think it's so odd because the follow-up dispute at its core is a contract dispute. The parties aren't debating anymore the merits of the federal claims. That's been resolved by the arbitrator. All the parties are addressing --

turns.name: BRETT KAVANAUGH
turns.text: There's still a pending federal case based on federal question jurisdiction.

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: That -- that's true. But, once -- once the arbitration is over and there is an arbitration award and we're at the stage where the court is considering a Section 9 or 10 application, the issue is not whether or not the federal claims have merit or not. That's already been resolved by the arbitrator. The issue is whether the arbitrator committed fraud, exceeded its powers, things that have nothing to do with the federal claims. And Badgerow's insight from our perspective is that at core is a contractual dispute.

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: Mr. Unikowsky --

turns.name: BRETT KAVANAUGH
turns.text: What should --

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: Yes?

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: I'm sorry, please.

turns.name: BRETT KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Go ahead. Go ahead. I just asked.

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: That was my question too. And I'm struggling to come up with a reason why Congress, in the Federal Arbitration Act, might have wanted diversity cases to remain in federal court for confirmation proceedings but federal question cases to go to state court. You're -- you're a very clever fellow. What -- what -- what -- what -- what might you imagine Congress's policy judgment would have been in that -- for that?

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: I think it's a similar policy judgment that underlay Badgerow. And I do recognize that in Badgerow there was no preexisting federal suit, but nonetheless, in Badgerow, the underlying question was federal. Like, the question was whether the arbitrator's resolution of federal claims complied with the Federal Arbitration Act, and the Court said that at core that's a contract dispute. And courts only have jurisdiction over contract disputes when the requirements for diversity of citizenship are satisfied, and so that's ultimately the distinction.

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: And, as a policy matter, I mean, what's happening in this case is, ultimately, the court is entering a judgment. Most -- the reason people typically seek to confirm arbitration awards is because they want a judgment they can execute on. That's why most confirm or vacate proceedings are when the plaintiff wins and they have a judgment and they want to use the authority of the court. And so, you know, state courts are very good at entering judgments and enforcing them. That's why Congress made a different decision in Section 8 cases, where the court seizes a ship, because, in that case, it makes perfect sense for the federal court to enter a judgment because it already has possession of the ship. So I think that's a sensible reason Congress would have said that we want the federal court to enter the judgment in the American courts.

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: Thank -- thank you. I think Justice Kavanaugh had more.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: So --

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: I'm sorry.

turns.name: BRETT KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Go ahead.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: -- am I right, Mr. Unikowsky, that your understanding of where Section 5 and 7 would continue to apply, it's really only in these diversity suits again?

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: I think so. But I think there's an argument possibly the other way that wouldn't apply in this case. So I think the correct answer, like, my ultimate bottom-line position is that's -- that, yes, there has to be an independent jurisdictional basis in Sections 5 and 7. You know, Section 4 has look-through jurisdiction. Section 8 has the anchor. And if such language isn't present, there needs to be an independent jurisdictional basis. Now I think there's a reasonable contrary argument that wouldn't apply here under the second head as Kokkonen puts it of -- of ancillary jurisdiction. There is an argument theoretically available that if the court compels arbitration but there's no arbitrator, appointing an arbitrator is a way of enforcing the order compelling arbitration. So I think a court could reach that conclusion, which wouldn't apply here because the arbitration is over. Like, the order compelling arbitration has been carried out. So I think the Court can reserve this question until the next time it has the opportunity to decide whether a particular type of arbitration --

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Here, the court didn't order arbitration because it thought it didn't have the power to, so --

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: That's correct. It stayed -- our argument --

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: So, under your theory, your answer to Justice Kagan is it doesn't have any power.

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: No, but I think, arguably, even in a case where the court stays pending arbitration like this one, the premise of the stay order is that the arbitration will happen. Otherwise, the arbitration will last forever. And so a court -- this is not my -- this is not my bottom-line answer, but I think a court could say that the court is in a sense enforcing the order staying arbitration by appointing an arbitrator to make sure the arbitration happens. That's just a way a court might avoid that result. But, ultimately, I think that the FAA says what it says. The court -- you know, Congress included jurisdictional language where one --

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: All right. Thank you, counsel.

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: Counsel, no one -- oh.

turns.name: BRETT KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Go ahead.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: No one, I think, disputes the court's jurisdiction to enter a consent decree. So why isn't confirmation of an arbitral -- arbitral award analogous since both involve taking a private resolution and making it a judgment of the court?

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: Because consent decrees require a degree of judicial involvement that doesn't exist when the court confirms an arbitration award. There's a rich body of law from this Court about when a court can enter a consent decree, and the Court has made clear that the court has to undertake an independent assessment of considerations such as whether or not the terms of the consent decree advance the objectives underlying the law at issue in the complaint and whether there's a sufficient nexus between the terms of the decree and the complaint. Obviously, there's a lot of discretion to the parties, but still, precisely because it's a judgment of the court, the court has to undertake an independent assessment of the terms. That doesn't really happen under the FAA.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: Is that just because it has been -- those kinds of assessments have been given to the arbitrator and sometimes even at the court's direction?

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: I don't think so. When the court confirms an arbitration award, the court isn't independently assessing, for example, whether the arbitrator's award furthers the goals of Title VII or the ADA or whatever federal statutory claims are being advanced. The court isn't sort of taking a second look at -- at the merits. All the court is doing is assessing whether the arbitrator exceeded its authority under the arbitration agreement and engaged in certain specified types of misconduct. So I think that the degree of judicial involvement under the -- on Section 9 and 10 is much less than what this Court has said is necessary to justify the court transforming a -- a pure settlement into a consent decree. So that's why I don't think that analogy carries over.

turns.name: BRETT KAVANAUGH
turns.text: The Second Circuit and the district court both thought that our statement in Cortez Byrd kind of made this simple because, there, we said, "We have, however, previously held that the court with the power to stay the action under Section 3 has the further power to confirm any ensuing arbitration award." So I know you're going to say that was out of context and -- but that statement on its own would resolve this, wouldn't it?

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: On its own, that statement is favorable to Respondents. And I'm going to say what you said I was going to say --

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: -- which is that if you read that whole paragraph, it's actually making a very different point which I think ultimately supports our position, which is that Congress wouldn't have intended a court's ability to -- to -- to decide a Section 9 or 10 application to turn on whether there was a preexisting suit on file, right? So the question in Cortez Byrd was one of venue, whether venue existed only in the jurisdiction in which the arbitration occurred or was broader than that. And the proponent of narrow venue conceded in that case that if there was a Section 3 stay, that court could exercise jurisdiction over the Section 9 and 10 application but argued for a different result if there was no Section 3 stay.

turns.name: BRETT KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Different question. Section 10 in state court. Thoughts?

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: Yes. So, I mean, we view Sections 9 and 10 as -- as joined at the hip. Like, Section 9 refers to confirming the award unless there's a Section 10 vacatur.

turns.name: BRETT KAVANAUGH
turns.text: The standards set forth in Section 10.

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: Oh. The Court reserved that question in -- in Badgerow.

turns.name: BRETT KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Yeah. What are your thoughts?

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: My thoughts are that it doesn't have to be exactly identical to the federal standard, but if the Court --

turns.name: BRETT KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Does it have to be close?

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: Pretty close. Like, if the court authorized de --

turns.name: BRETT KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Pretty close?

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: Yeah. Basically, pretty close. I mean, the Court hasn't addressed this. Look, if the state court authorizes de novo review of an arbitration award --

turns.name: BRETT KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Because the state court theoretically could have very different standards. Then the arbitration award's thrown out. But then the new suit, again, back to Justice Thomas's first question -- not the new suit -- the suit comes back to federal court then after the state court's thrown out the arbitration award.

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: So, typically, that's not --

turns.name: BRETT KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Isn't that a strange system?

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: I don't think it would come back to federal court. Typically, what would happen is they would arbitrate the claim again. Like, the effect of, for instance --

turns.name: BRETT KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Well, it depends what the state court said in tossing the arbitration award, right?

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: Well, not really because, if the federal court -- if the case is arbitrable, then -- and the state court says there's some defect in the arbitration proceeding, but the case is still arbitrable, you'd presumably go back and rearbitrate. Theoretically, the parties, for example, might stipulate that if the award is vacated, they're just going to give up on arbitration, and then they could go back to federal court, either file a Rule 60 motion, or the federal court could opt to wait to dismiss the case until the state court's proceedings are done and it could be reactivated in federal court.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Do states have pretty close standards?

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: They do. I don't think that's -- I'm not aware of any state that diverges significantly. Like, there's, like, minor wording differences sometimes. I think there would be an obvious preemption problem. As -- as I said, if the state authorized de novo review of an arbitration award, that would be contrary to Section 2's substantive standard that arbitration agreements should be enforced. But, if there's, like, very small distinctions or differences in things like the statutes of limitations, I don't think that those types of differences would necessarily --

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: I have another question also just about practice here. Are -- are most of these 9 and 10 applications brought as freestanding applications, or are they connected to a -- to a federal suit in -- in the way that this one was?

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: I mean, I've read a lot of cases. I'm -- I'm not sure of the empirics there. I think they're mostly brought independently because, usually, there's not that much dispute over whether parties have to arbitrate, but I've seen it on both sides. I -- I don't know a specific number.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: And is there a way in which your theory or Mr. Geyser's theory would push the practice in one direction or another in a way that either made sense or didn't make sense?

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: So, yes. I appreciate the opportunity to say that. So one of the concerns about Respondents' position is that it'll create an incentive every -- in every case to file a freestanding motion to compel arbitration or do something else to get to federal court if you want to have that anchor. And this Court remarked on this in both Vaden and as well as Justice Breyer's dissent in Badgerow. Vaden said it would be a totally artificial distinction between -- to distinguish between Section 9 and 10 applications associated with preexisting suits and not so associated. And Justice Breyer's Badgerow dissent reiterated that language that the jurisdictional anchor would create that totally artificial distinction.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. Justice Thomas, anything further? Justice Alito?

turns.name: SAMUEL ALITO
turns.text: Well, just out of curiosity, do you think we should ask Claude to decide this case?

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: No. I -- I adhere to the wise judgment of -- of this Court.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Sotomayor?

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: You're suggesting to me that people who have stays in place typically don't move that court for motions to confirm the award, that they usually go to -- to -- somewhere else for that?

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: No. I'm suggesting that usually, motions to confirm the award aren't necessary, especially when there was --

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: No, no, no, I understand that. But, when there is a stay, what's the number of cases that go to another court?

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: Well, there's a circuit conflict on this issue. And it's true that most of the courts have gone with Respondents' position, but most of those opinions are sort of not -- not very -- there's not a lot of reasoning --

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: It doesn't matter, but the -- the vast majority -- I'm not aware of a Section 3 stay case where the other side has come to a different court to confirm the award.

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: Well, I mean, there's a -- there's a circuit conflict on this issue. And --

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: So the -- where they're not permitted -- that's what drew us to take this case. Where they're not permitted to, they can't.

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: But the point is, when they are permitted, they stay in the court where the stay is granted, correct?

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: Right. It's likely that if the Court rules for Respondents, people don't want to pay a second filing fee --

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: All right.

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: -- so they'll probably go --

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: You -- you talk about your fear of -- of people choosing litigation, choosing a venue, and not arbitrating in order to get a venue.

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: You have to be Houdini and -- well, not Houdini. You have to have magical powers to know whether you're going to win or lose, right?

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: No. You can just bring a freestanding Section 4 motion to compel arbitration, which this Court held you could do in Vaden.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Well, you didn't bring it.

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: No.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: You had to have the foresight -- you went to New York, a jurisdiction that had no connection to the dispute where -- where the dispute happened, and only because you lost in New York are you wanting to get out of New York.

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: Your Honor, the facts of this specific case don't present the practical concern you've elaborated --

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Well, but they present the practical concern that your fear requires a lot of predictions that parties are unlikely to engage in because, right now, most of them don't fight where they're going. They just do arbitration.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: And most of them don't fight awards. They accept them.

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: Right, but the point is, look, if I'm -- if I am advising a client and the client says we're about to arbitrate and we might need down the road to file an application to confirm or vacate the award, and the client says, I'd rather be in federal court, in every case, I would say let's go to federal court.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: You know, I practiced a lot of arbitration law. I don't know a client who ever said that to me. I want to file -- file it in this court in case my -- I win the award, I want to be in federal court. Thank you, counsel.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Kagan? Justice Gorsuch? Justice Kavanaugh? Justice Barrett?

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: Just as a practical matter, under your theory, the stay is in place. You have the award. You want to confirm it. Do you have to go get a -- do you file a motion to dismiss first in the district court before you go to the state court to try -- state court to try to get the arbitral award confirmed or vacated?

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: No, I don't think you necessarily have to do that. I don't think there's a specific order of operations because we view this as a non- -- like, the arbitration award as a non-jurisdictional defense, akin to res judicata or release. I think the federal court could opt, if it knows that there's an ongoing vacate or confirm proceeding, it could opt to just wait to dismiss the federal suit until those proceedings wrap up. I don't think there's necessarily an order of operations that's required.

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: Okay. Thanks.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Jackson?

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: Can I just take you back to the original questions that I think Justice Sotomayor and Justice Kagan asked about treatment of this as a motion --

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: -- and Section 6. And I know that you referenced Badgerow's statement that the applications will get streamlined treatment as a motion, but Badgerow cited a case called Hall Street Associates for that proposition. It quoted from Hall Street. And that entire quote says, "will get streamlined treatment as a motion obviating the separate contract action that would usually be necessary to enforce or tinker with an arbitral award in court." So it appears at least as of Hall Street, the -- the Court understood the way that this worked, that the reason why you're treating this as a motion is that it would mean that you wouldn't need another contract action in another court in order to enforce the award. Why -- why doesn't this undermine the argument that you're making now?

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: Because I -- I interpret that language in Hall and Badgerow differently. Section 6 does not distinguish between applications in a freestanding case and applications that are associated with a preexisting case. It's just a single rule that covers all FAA applications that says that they'll be treated like a motion. And what the Court said in Badgerow was what that means is not that it is a motion in a case in which there is jurisdiction. It'll just be treated like one.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: I understand, but in Hall, it says the reason why it's treated as a motion is because it will obviate the separate contract action. And what I'm confused about is how that could be consistent with your vision, which would require a separate contract.

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: Well, I think there has to be jurisdiction over the contract action. If there isn't jurisdiction, the court couldn't hear it. And if there is jurisdiction, then, yes, you could -- the court could hear it as a motion and it would save a lot of time and effort. And Section 9 and 10 have these very specific service-of-process provisions. And if the defendant is within the district, it's sort of easier to serve process than it is normally. And I think Section 6 is similar with that streamlined procedure, but that doesn't mean there is jurisdiction. It simply talks about what happens if there is jurisdiction.

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. Mr. Geyser.

turns.name: GEYSER
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: Consistent with decades of established doctrine, federal courts with preexisting jurisdiction may resolve related claims in the same pending case, and nothing in the FAA or Badgerow overrides the ordinary jurisdictional rules in this setting. A federal court already vested with original jurisdiction has obvious power to hear FAA motions in that pending action. There's no need to ask if there is a redundant basis for original jurisdiction because jurisdiction already exists, and any FAA motion falls squarely within a court's supplemental jurisdiction. This follows under the plain text of Title 28. It adheres to ordinary jurisdictional principles, and it promotes the FAA's purpose and objectives, including facilitating the judiciary's supervisory role in enforcing awards. Petitioner's contrary approach contravenes Section 1367. It is at odds with Badgerow, and it invites unworkable and untenable results. Respondents are simply asking to go back to the same court to enter judgment and dismiss Petitioner's case, without having to bifurcate these proceedings, file new state law litigation, and endure a parallel federal appeal and chase down Petitioner to finally bring this case to a close. I welcome the Court's questions.

turns.name: CLARENCE THOMAS
turns.text: How exactly does 1367 apply to a Section 9 application where there are no apparent claims?

turns.name: GEYSER
turns.text: Where the --

turns.name: CLARENCE THOMAS
turns.text: Where the claims in a section -- in an application under Section 9?

turns.name: GEYSER
turns.text: Well, I think it applies exactly under Section 1367. You have an existing federal case which vests original jurisdiction. The Section 9 claim, if you look at it as a state law analog, as a contractual resolution of the claim, as Badgerow did, then it -- there would be supplemental jurisdiction over that -- that state law claim. Now --

turns.name: CLARENCE THOMAS
turns.text: But it's not a state claim.

turns.name: GEYSER
turns.text: It -- it isn't, which I think is all the more reason that there clearly is jurisdiction under 1367. 1367 isn't limited to state law actions. It's any claim that's so related to the original claim that it forms part of the same case or controversy.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Well, but it's the same nucleus of operative fact. That's the -- that's the test. How is this the same nucleus of operative fact? It seems to me completely two different sets of facts.

turns.name: GEYSER
turns.text: Well, I don't think so, Your Honor. It's the -- it's the contractual resolution of the pending claim in the same case.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Well, but, Mr. Geyser, I mean, what -- what the -- I think Mr. Unikowsky said this -- what the -- the -- the second case is going to be about is something like did the arbitrator commit fraud. It's going to have nothing to do with the actual claims in the case. You're not allowed to relitigate those.

turns.name: GEYSER
turns.text: You -- you -- you're not, but I don't think that matters. I don't think the test is are the elements coterminous. It's do they -- are the claims so related they form part of the same case or controversy. The arbitration award is an affirmative defense in the case. It's hard to see how something can be an affirmative defense to the claim in the case without being related to that case.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Yeah. I think that that's just not 1367's focus. 1367 says we're -- we're not going to look sort of as an abstract matter as to whether two claims were brought by the same parties or have certain features or are connected with each other in a certain way, other than one particular way, which is are the facts the same in these two claims. And the facts are not going to be the same in these two claims.

turns.name: GEYSER
turns.text: Well -- well, not all the facts, but -- but you're still asking -- this is a contractual resolution of the claim that is pending. It's like saying that the judgment in a case is somehow unrelated to the original claim that produced the judgment. I -- I think it doesn't have to be the same. We -- we absolutely -- I agree with my friend that these claims will not overlap. Often, they have nothing to do with each other, except --

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Yeah, common nucleus of operative fact. And often they have nothing to do with each other. I would say almost always they have nothing to do with each other.

turns.name: GEYSER
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: How could that possibly be a common nucleus of operative fact?

turns.name: GEYSER
turns.text: Well, it doesn't -- it's not that every fact has to share the same, you know, original atom. It's the, is there a common nucleus of operative fact?

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: It's the great-great-grandchild of the original claim kind of tenuated on? I mean, what -- is it a claim? I mean, you -- you characterize it as an affirmative defense. Not a counterclaim. Not a subsidiary claim. It doesn't seem like it's a claim.

turns.name: GEYSER
turns.text: Well, it's -- it's resolving -- I think it is resolving the claim in the sense that it is -- it is contractually, you know, disposing of the claim that has been brought. Now I will say that there's an entirely different way to look at this. And part of the problem is that the FAA, as this Court has acknowledged, is unique. We have an unusual situation where we have a federal right under a federal statute that doesn't give rise to federal jurisdiction. And one analog is to say that the arbitration award is like a contractual settlement. So then confirming the award under the FAA, even though it's a federal statute, is like resolving a state law contract dispute, sort of like Kokkonen. Another analog, though, is just to say this is the federal directive on how a court decides what to do when the claim that's pending before it is now settled. So, in the same way that Congress can provide rules of decision in Rule 23 to say, if you're going to settle a class action, do you accept the settlement or not, or attach conditions to Rule 41, or do exactly what the Court contemplated could have happened in Kokkonen --

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: And if that's the way you're looking at it, are you drawing on original jurisdiction then?

turns.name: GEYSER
turns.text: Yes. It's --

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: I mean, that's not -- that's not a supplemental jurisdiction idea.

turns.name: GEYSER
turns.text: The -- then we don't need supplemental jurisdiction --

turns.name: KETANJI BROWN JACKSON
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: GEYSER
turns.text: -- which is why we offered two competing paths in our brief that lead to effectively the same result.

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: And it's not ancillary jurisdiction, just to clarify. You're saying that the original jurisdiction, the 1331 hook is carrying you all the way through?

turns.name: GEYSER
turns.text: I think that is one way, I think, and a sensible way to look at it. I think it's actually the way that -- that Cortez Byrd was contemplating, saying, of course, once you have an original case and you've stayed it under Section 3, then, of course, you have jurisdiction to resolve what's a motion in the pending case, just as Justice Kagan and Justice Jackson pointed out, to decide what to do with that contractual resolution of the single pending claim.

turns.name: BRETT KAVANAUGH
turns.text: You heard his response on Cortez Byrd. Do you want to -- your friend. You want to react to that?

turns.name: GEYSER
turns.text: Sure. So I think -- I -- I think my friend is correct that Cortez Byrd was citing Marine Transit, which is a Section 8 case, but Cortez Byrd was not a Section 8 case. So the way I read Cortez Byrd and I think the way the courts have read Cortez Byrd is the Court was importing this common-sense intuitive proposition that happened to arise in the Section 8 context and putting it into the Section 9 through 11 context. And Cortez Byrd, by the way, was a 2002 decision. It's been decades on the books. Courts have construed it exactly the same way. It was only until the Fourth Circuit --

turns.name: BRETT KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Most of the courts have gone that way, right --

turns.name: GEYSER
turns.text: Well, I think --

turns.name: BRETT KAVANAUGH
turns.text: -- most of the courts of appeals?

turns.name: GEYSER
turns.text: I -- I think every court has gone that way. The only one that hasn't was the Fourth Circuit, which, with -- with all due respect to the Fourth Circuit, I think just profoundly misread Badgerow.

turns.name: BRETT KAVANAUGH
turns.text: That's why I said "most."

turns.name: GEYSER
turns.text: Yeah. And -- and that -- that is -- and, in fact, when we say "most," that -- that is effectively as far as we're aware the only court that has gone the other way. I'm not aware of a single court before Badgerow that's gone the other way.

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: Mr. Geyser, you've laid out two paths for us, one, the supplemental jurisdiction path, reliant on a statute that could never be accused of being overly precise. The other would be the original jurisdiction path. I know what your answer's going to be, and it's going to be they're both great, Your Honor, but which one do you think, if forced to choose, is -- is the safer route and why?

turns.name: GEYSER
turns.text: I mean, I -- I really do think that they're both great and I do think that they both work.

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: I knew -- I --

turns.name: GEYSER
turns.text: So the --

turns.name: NEIL GORSUCH
turns.text: Any -- any -- any lawyer who -- who -- who doesn't say that isn't worth his salt where you wish to win, and I understand that. But --

turns.name: GEYSER
turns.text: I -- I think that -- and it really is that it's -- it's half -- you know, half in one hand, half in the other. It's -- I would say that you could say it's part of the original jurisdiction. That's just a very easy way to resolve it. We're in the same pending case. We're trying to dispose of the original claims.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Your brief -- if I read your brief, your brief is a supplemental jurisdiction brief.

turns.name: GEYSER
turns.text: Well, we -- we did make both arguments, and we did supplemental jurisdiction --

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: I mean, you made one in a couple of paragraphs, and you made the other in 50 pages.

turns.name: GEYSER
turns.text: Well, we thought one --

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: That might be a slight exaggeration.

turns.name: GEYSER
turns.text: Well, no. We thought one was so obvious that we were trying to help the Court if it decided to go the other way. But I -- I do think, though, that the reason that we were fronting 1367 is -- is because of the analogy that the Court drew in Badgerow, is that if you do think of this as a contractual resolution of the claim and then you think of it as a dispute over the -- that contractual settlement, then it -- it does look like it's a little bit different. And I do think that they clearly arise out of the same ultimate, you know, common nucleus of operative fact. It's clearly the same case or controversy because, again, it is -- it is literally resolving the single controversy before the case, which is why, if you looked at how courts typically dispose of these actions, if they do retain jurisdiction, as every court outside the Fourth Circuit does, is that they say we're going to -- if we confirm the arbitration award, you enter judgment on the arbitration award and judgment on the underlying claim. You do it in one fell swoop because it is effectively the same action.

turns.name: BRETT KAVANAUGH
turns.text: Can you respond to your friend's comments, picking up on Justice Breyer's dissent in Badgerow about incentives?

turns.name: GEYSER
turns.text: The -- I think that it's really hard to see how there's an incentive to file an anchor suit for any number of reasons, including that courts -- just parties aren't doing that. Again, Cortez Byrd's language, you know, my friend can quibble whether it was overstated or improper, but Cortez Byrd's language was unequivocal. If you imported that -- that sentence into this decision, you could write a one-page opinion with a holding that says we resolved this in Cortez Byrd. That was in 2002, and there hasn't been a single identified case of someone filing an anchor suit, and I think it's for similar reasons that Justice Sotomayor pointed out. It's a very odd litigant who's choosing an original venue not to litigate the case and not to compel -- not to compel arbitration, which is sometimes heavily contested, and that's what you'd be worried about, but thinking months or years down the road, what is this going to look like once we get that award? Do I want to confirm it or vacate it? And I'd rather be in federal court, so I'm going to file this preemptive federal action. And for some parties, it's not even an option. If you're a plaintiff and you file the original lawsuit to litigate, then the -- it's not even clear. You might have -- first, under Morgan versus Sundance, you might have forfeited your arbitration rights because you're trying to litigate. And if the defendant doesn't move to compel arbitration, you're then out of luck. I mean, you -- you showed up there. It's -- it's your lawsuit. So I -- I don't think that those incentives actually play out. And if you're concerned about the practical consequences, I would look at the practical consequences of where my friend's position would lead courts and litigants. It would create this -- this odd situation where a party finishes in federal court after the stay. Now -- now, apparently, they have to -- the federal court will either dismiss the case without knowing whether those claims are actually resolved or not, or they stay the case to wait, as my friend said, potentially several years for the post-arbitration motions to play out in state court. Now, if the court -- if the federal court does dismiss the case, now we have an even worse situation. Now we have parallel proceedings, you have a federal appeal potentially contesting the pre-arbitration motion to compel arbitration because you can't take that until you have a final judgment in federal court. And then you have the same litigants going to state court to challenge what happened in the arbitration itself. So, instead of having one proceeding up and running where parties have already paid a filing fee, they've found the defendant, they've effectuated service, they've maybe thought about venue, they thought about personal jurisdiction, everything is up and running where you can resolve it in one proceeding, take one appeal -- it's nice, neat, and tidy -- instead, you have now competing litigation in separate tracks in different court systems. I -- I think -- I don't think that's what Congress intended. I think that would create what Cortez Byrd described as, you know, attributing to Congress a taste for the bizarre, and I do think that would be a bizarre situation.

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: I agree with you that it seems more practical and tidy to do it the way that you just described. I'm wondering if you fronted the 1367 argument because, in some ways, it provides -- I mean, I still struggle with the common nucleus of operative fact and exactly where is the claim issues, but it provides a theoretical grounding. When I'm trying to think about 1331 just by itself, I'm trying to figure out what in 1331, if I'm blinded, I'm taking 1367 out of it, justifies the adjudication of a separate contract dispute.

turns.name: GEYSER
turns.text: I think it would be exactly the same way that when a court invokes, say, Rule 23(e), it doesn't say do I have separate jurisdiction to decide whether to accept this settlement or not, or whether, in the Rule 41 context, do I have jurisdiction to decide whether to retain, you know, jurisdiction over the settlement to police it or to put it in the decree. I think -- I think it is part of the -- the original power to dispose of the claim.

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: And so, under that formulation, we're thinking of it more as a motion than a claim, which would be inconsistent with the 1367 theory, right?

turns.name: GEYSER
turns.text: Debatably. I think -- 1367, I think, has -- has been described as -- as quite broad. I think it's pretty capacious. I think it can cover lots of things. But, luckily for us, Section 6 under the FAA says treat these as motions. So, if -- if you want to treat the -- the -- this as a motion covered by original jurisdiction, you have a very easy path to do it. It's consistent with Cortez Byrd. It's consistent with the fact that Section 6 says treat this as a motion. I think it's even consistent with Kokkonen. And, again -- and, again --

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: Well --

turns.name: GEYSER
turns.text: -- just to be clear, Kokkonen was dealing with a far more aggressive assertion of ancillary jurisdiction. That was a dismissed case, and the parties were trying to get the court to reinvoke jurisdiction to decide something.

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: Okay. Let's talk about Kokkonen. What about Kokkonen's kind of Bucket 2, where Kokkonen says, well, it would be different if the court had reserved in the dismissal the authority to enforce the terms of the settlement? You didn't push that particularly hard. Why?

turns.name: GEYSER
turns.text: Well, I -- we did, and I'm sorry if it wasn't clear that we did. Here, it's even better. You don't -- you don't have a dismissal. You have existing pending claims in a pending case. So Kokkonen, again, is a much harder case. There, the Court's saying, I'm going to dismiss this action and I'm going to retain jurisdiction just in case something happens down the road. Here, we have pending federal claims. And if you -- and you don't even know what to do with these claims. So I think, with all due respect to my friend, I think he's incorrect. To dismiss the case without knowing whether the arbitration award will ultimately stand doesn't make any sense. If the award is ultimately vacated, the parties could very well say, you know what, that was a waste of time, let's just litigate in court.

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: So is ancillary jurisdiction of the sort -- ancillary enforcement jurisdiction of the sort that Kokkonen talks about applicable here? Is that a coherent way to think about this?

turns.name: GEYSER
turns.text: I -- I think you could. Now I -- I do think that this raises a slight complexity about -- to the extent 1367 has displaced or captured ancillary jurisdiction, but I think the very fact that Kokkonen contemplated that a court could retain jurisdiction to decide those subsequent settlement disputes suggests that this would be covered then by 1367.

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: Well, that's why I said enforcement jurisdiction. I think that's different because nobody really disputes that a court could exercise enforcement -- ancillary enforcement jurisdiction over, say, a contempt proceeding. So that would be different. It wouldn't be subsumed by 1367 the same way.

turns.name: GEYSER
turns.text: We -- we think that works perfectly well, and I think it's -- it's a sensible way to look at this. I do want to address just very quickly the Section 8 argument about does the text of Section 8 somehow preclude jurisdiction under 1331 or 1332 or 1367, and I think it quite clearly doesn't. Section 8 is a maritime provision, and it starts off by saying, notwithstanding anything else going on, so this is Congress's attempt to say we're creating a self-contained unit to deal specifically with admiralty and we're going to provide specific rules for that context. In order for that to preclude the assertion of jurisdiction elsewhere -- because, again, we're not relying on the FAA for jurisdiction; we're relying on Title 28 -- it would have to show that Congress in Section 8 was thinking, I'm going to foreclose jurisdiction for every non-maritime case, the overwhelming swath of cases that arise under the FAA, simply by creating a specific rule of decision for that narrow context.

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: Mr. Geyser, just going back to this, oh, it's just a motion in a case, if you have jurisdiction in a case, of course, you have jurisdiction over this too, I mean, this is a very unusual kind of motion, right? It's a motion which could have been brought as a freestanding suit, which is -- which is essentially a freestanding suit and a freestanding suit in which there would have been no jurisdiction. So notwithstanding that there's this sort of, like, unusual posture in which it appears as, like, a motion in another suit, we all know that it's a different suit and a different suit in which there is no jurisdiction. So why do you -- why does that carry you home?

turns.name: GEYSER
turns.text: Well, I -- I think it's -- it's not quite just an entirely different suit because, again, it's -- it's a motion with a different nature of claim to resolve the original pending claim. So I -- I do think, again, just like Rule 23(e) is not a different suit, the -- the parties settle class-wide --

turns.name: ELENA KAGAN
turns.text: I think what I mean of it as a different suit is it's a bunch of contract claims. Let's say it's about, like, the arbitrator committed fraud and so my -- the contract is invalid. That's what this suit is. That's what this motion is. Call it whatever you want. That's what this thing is. And that has nothing to do with the employment action that's kicked off this whole thing.

turns.name: GEYSER
turns.text: Except for the fact that it is resolving the employment action that kicked off this whole thing. So, again, that's why I think you'd be -- it's really hard to say that resolving a claim is unrelated to the claim, even if the reason you resolved it, maybe you settled because they give you -- they offered you a lot of money or they -- they gave you something that you wanted. That has nothing to do with the underlying factual allegations. It's still resolving the claim. And that -- I think that shows why it's part of the same case or controversy. I mean, it is literally resolving the case or controversy. And -- and, again, I think a contrary view on that would say that an affirmative defense to a claim in a case doesn't arise or relate to that same case, which -- which it -- it's hard to even articulate the concept because it just doesn't make much sense. But, again, I think that's why they're -- we tried to offer two different paths in the brief because depending on which way you look at it, we're dealing with this odd hybrid that the FAA set up. Again, no -- no one has ever applauded Congress for creating, you know, the world's cleanest statute. It's five pages of the U.S. Code. I think this Court is burdened with two or three cases a year, it seems, under this statute going back I don't know how many years. But looking at it here, I think what Cortez Byrd acknowledged, you know, two decades ago still holds. Of course, if you have jurisdiction to stay the case under Section 3, you have jurisdiction to process it at the end of the day.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. Justice Thomas, anything further? Justice Alito? Justice Kagan? Justice Gorsuch? Justice Barrett, anything further? Justice Jackson? Thank you, counsel.

turns.name: GEYSER
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Rebuttal, Mr. Unikowsky?

turns.name: UNIKOWSKY
turns.text: Thank you, Your Honor. I'd like to first begin with the discussion of Section 1331, which didn't occupy much of the briefing but did occupy much of the discussion this morning. I don't think that confirming the arbitration award can be justified under Section 1331 because that judgment is not the resolution of the original claim. The original claim was a federal employment claim, and the resolution of that claim is that the claim cannot proceed in federal court because the parties agreed to arbitrate that claim. So just as a court dismisses a claim based on res judicata or release when it turns out that the parties have adjudicated the case in a different forum, the appropriate resolution of a federal claim is to dismiss it because the federal court can't hear it when it's being decided elsewhere. But confirming the arbitration award is different. It's transforming the arbitration award into a judgment, and the practical difference there is that it allows Respondents to execute a money judgment against Petitioner. So what happened here is that the parties agreed to arbitrate, and then this arbitrator entered the sanctions award, which is a money judgment against Petitioner, and then confirming the award transforms that contractual resolution of the claim into a federal judgment. And that's not a federal judgment. The money judgment against Petitioner, which is the real practical effect of confirming this award, is not resolution of the original claim. It's transforming the parties' agreement to adjudicate this in a contractual method into a federal judgment. That's why I think extra jurisdiction is needed. Section 1331 doesn't do the trick. And so then you get to Section 1367, and as some of the questions from the bench today made clear, there is no common nucleus effect. I think Kokkonen is directly on point on this issue. I understand, in Kokkonen, there is a dismissal rather than a stay, but as to the first head, as Kokkonen called it, of ancillary jurisdiction, the Court said it "permits 'disposition by a single court of claims that are in varying respects and degrees factually interdependent,'" which was very similar to the 1367 analysis. And then the Court held that the facts underlying respondent's dismissed claim for a breach of agency agreement and those underlying its claim for breach of settlement agreement have nothing to do with each other. It would neither be necessary nor even particularly efficient that they'd be adjudicated together. And that reasoning is right on point here because, at core, the arbitration award is similar to a breach-of-settlement-agreement claim. It's a -- it's a contractual way of resolving a dispute. And the Court insight from Kokkonen is parties that agree to resolve a dispute via contract who later dispute that contractual resolution are adjudicating a different dispute from the underlying federal dispute. I'd like to say a few words about Cortez Byrd. You know, the Court has said that -- the Court has cautioned against, you know, giving too much credit to drive-by jurisdictional rulings. And I think this is just the canonical case of a drive-by jurisdictional ruling. So, first of all, jurisdiction was conceded in that case under diversity jurisdiction. Also, there was no Section 3 stay at all. So the jurisdictional anchor issue was doubly not presented. Meanwhile, the Court was talking about what it had previously held in this case, Marine Transit, that everyone agrees is irrelevant. The Court was also talking about a venue issue which was also conceded by one side in that discussion. And all of this in service of making an argument that's ultimately the argument we're making in this very case, which is that Congress would not have intended for a court's authority to hear a Section 9 or 10 stay application to depend on whether or not there is a preexisting suit on file. So I do understand that lots of lower courts have followed that statement from Cortez Byrd, which makes sense. I think, when a lower court sees a sentence like that in the U.S. reports, much like in the Royal Canin case, which presented a very similar scenario, the courts will often go along with it. That's essentially what happened here. But I think that, you know, this Court has, you know, the authority to check, as it did in Royal Canin, whether one sentence really establishes a holding. And I think it just doesn't when you look at the posture of that case as compared to this one. Finally, in terms of the practical effects that Respondents identify, I don't think that they're that -- that significant. I mean, what happens is, after there's an arbitration award, you go to the federal court and if a party says, look, we're planning to go to state court to confirm the award or vacate it, the federal court can say, look, okay, I'll wait to see whether or not the case ever comes back and I'll just delay the dismissal a little bit longer and they'll go to state court and -- and litigate there. I mean, you know, parallel federal and state litigation happens every day in our system of -- of -- in our -- in our federal system, and I don't think that the practical concerns here are any greater than they would be in any other case when you have parallel litigation across the federal and state systems. If there's no further questions, we'd ask the Court to reverse the judgment below.

turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you, counsel. The case is submitted.
